ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general snail food product class; no specific entry for apple snail with lemongrass steamed preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJN covers freshwater snails as a commodity; no specific code for the lemongrass-steamed preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for apple snail or freshwater snail food product in clinical terminology.

serving.description: 1 portion (approximately 200 g edible snail meat with aromatics)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of apple snail with lemongrass is approximately 200 grams, representing a shared starter or individual snack portion as commonly served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Snails, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17325); lemongrass and aromatics adjustment based on FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for apple snail with lemongrass exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw snail data (NDB 17325 / FDC 174228) as the primary protein and fat anchor. Lemongrass and aromatic herbs contribute negligible macronutrients at typical seasoning quantities. Sodium is elevated to reflect fish sauce seasoning typical of this preparation, estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA condiment data. Values represent the edible snail meat portion per 100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of high-protein, low-carbohydrate shellfish and mollusc foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for shellfish and molluscs
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for apple snail with lemongrass. Apple snails are high-protein, very low-carbohydrate molluscs (approximately 2 g carbohydrate per 100 g). By analogy with other shellfish and molluscs in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values in the range of 0–10 due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content, a GI of 5 is assigned. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 4.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.2, classified as very low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Apple snail with lemongrass contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g due to fish sauce used in preparation; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced fish sauce to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 2 g of carbohydrate per 100 g and a very low estimated glycemic index of 5, apple snail with lemongrass has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal, though sodium from fish sauce should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high protein, moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Apple snail with lemongrass is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.2 g per 100 g) and provides lean protein, making it a heart-friendly protein source; however, the sodium content from fish sauce seasoning warrants moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apple Snail with Lemongrass, known in Vietnamese as Ốc Bươu Hấp Sả (romanized: Oc Buou Hap Sa), is a Vietnamese freshwater snail dish prepared by steaming live apple snails (Pomacea canaliculata) with aromatic lemongrass stalks, garlic, chilli, ginger, and kaffir lime leaves, then typically served with a dipping sauce of fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, and sugar. The dish is a popular street food and restaurant appetiser in Vietnam and is also served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore, such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The snail meat is firm, slightly chewy, and mildly briny, with the lemongrass imparting a fragrant citrus-herbal aroma that neutralises any muddy undertones. Nutritionally, apple snail meat is a high-protein, low-fat, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 16 g of protein and only 90 kcal per 100 g, making it an excellent lean protein source. It is also a good source of calcium, iron, phosphorus, and magnesium. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to fish sauce used in preparation and dipping sauces. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 5), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for those with mollusc or fish allergies. It is gluten-free and dairy-free but is not halal due to the use of non-halal snails in traditional preparation.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for apple snail with lemongrass exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw snail data (FDC 174228) as the closest approved source anchor. Sodium is estimated upward to reflect fish sauce seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database shellfish/mollusc category data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw snails (FDC 174228) as no direct approved-source entry for apple snail with lemongrass exists. Values represent edible snail meat per 100 g. 2. Sodium_mg (420 mg) is estimated upward from the raw snail baseline (~70 mg/100 g) to reflect fish sauce seasoning typical of this preparation, based on FAO/INFOODS SEA condiment data. 3. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy snail data; snail meat contains no free sugars. 4. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy snail data; mollusc meat contains no dietary fibre. 5. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no hydrogenated fats are present in this preparation. 6. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy snail entry. 7. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. 8. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. 9. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. 10. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. 11. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this food. 12. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for apple snail exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 13. Halal status is marked false as traditional Vietnamese snail preparation may not follow halal slaughter requirements; restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified. 14. The cuisine tag in the input was 'Singapore' reflecting the GrabFood Singapore source; the dish is Vietnamese in origin and has been reclassified accordingly. 15. Price data (SGD 20.00) from GrabFood Singapore is preserved as context but not used in nutritional calculations.
